© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
Launch Summary:
▪ Will Falconer, DVM - Veterinarian for 35+ years… specialty - homeopathic medicine
▪ Email list: approximately 8,000
▪ Existing product: e-book and Audio book bundle for $45 (“Vital Animals Don’t Get
Heartworms!” The Drug Free Prevention Program That Works)
▪ Quick Launch: Offered a “nearly 50% off” sale on an e-book/audiobook combo that had
been slowly selling for over a year. Some days one or two sales, some days none.
▪ New bundle: Added (to the e-book and audio book) a bonus Special Report and a one-
hour Q&A webinar.
▪ Special offer price: $25
▪ Deliver: Sent 6 emails over 4 days - Over the Memorial Day holiday weekend (in the
U.S.)
▪ Results: Total Sales: $8845 - 555 total orders
Day 1: $ 875 - 35 orders
Day 2: $ 1600 - 133 orders
Day 3: $ 3550 - 203 orders
Day 4: $ 2300 - 141 orders
Day 5: $ 520 - 43 additional orders at full price with no bonuses
day after offer closed
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
Communication Strategy
1. Before the Quick Launch…
• Shot Across the Bow 1 week before in his regular e-newsletter (small paragraph in full
newsletter)
2. 1st Email – Opening Email: Telling the Story
• Sent 6:30 pm on May 28th
• Subject line: Unusual Times Call for Unusual Strategies
• MAKING IT WORK: “Sent way later than intended due to tech challenges: pages (this
was sent w/o even a landing page!, link was just the coupon!), but TY page, coupon, new
product that RM could track and make members from, and CK could segment.”
3. 2nd Email: Regular Newsletter
• Sent May 29th at 7:48 pm, so 25 hours after the first email.
• Gave a brief recounting and sent interested peeps to a landing page with the first letter
on it
4. 3rd Email: Purely marketing
• Sent the day after newsletter, plain text only, no logo header even; sent May 30th, 8:30
am, when he *thought* he’d close at midnight.
• (“That changed, thanks to a friend for the inspiration that closing on Memorial Day was
dumb.”)
5. 4th Email: “Last Call”
• Sent 5:30 pm May 30th
• Subject line: Courtesy Alert: Closing Soon
• (This is when he *thought* he was closing that night, “but stayed tuned. Remember Jeff:
“When you’re in the launch, you can change things!”)
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 10 --

4
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
6. 5th Email: Changing my mind – extending one more day
• This one went out early am the morning after Memorial Day, May 31st
• Subject line: Duh. Just Duh.
7. 6th Email: Truly Ending Tonight
• This turned out to be the “real” last email for this Quick Launch - Sent May 31st, day
after Memorial Day, 6pm
• Subject Line: Last Courtesy Alert: Closing Soon

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
Okay. I've got a great case study for you. This is a Quick Launch case study
with Will Falconer.
The great thing about Quick Launch is, is that you can bring in a lot of money
with almost, with very very little effort. It is for people that already have a business. You
already have a list. You already have an offer. Basically, what you're going to put together
a special offer, call it a sale.
I don't like to put things on sale. I go to great lengths to avoid having sales. I
think, in general, when you start putting stuff on sale regularly, all you've done, is you've
reduced your prices.
In the online environment, it's basically you're always in a battle of maintaining
your perceived value. If you're selling training products, it's hard for people to judge the
value of your product, so you have to tell them what the value is. You go to great lengths
to establish what the value is. Then, if all of a sudden, you start discounting your
products, then it establishes a new value. It hurts your positioning.
Basically, people are only going to buy from you ... people that are on your list.
It's not like they're just driving by. You know, you're driving down the highway and you
see the furniture store has got a 50% off sale, and you're like, "Oh wow, I'm going to
going check that out." You don't realize they have that sale on every single week.
When you have an email list, they're going to be watching your emails. If you put
something on sale every single week, they're going to know it. So, in general, I like to
avoid having sales.
The Quick Launch is basically a way to have a sale, because you
introduce a reason why and you tell a story behind that reason why. It's not
something you want to do often, but whenever you do need a quick surge of sales,
it's really cool. You're going to see how Will did it here. He did a really nice job with it.
Will's business is, he's a vet. He teaches ... his specialty is homeopathic medicine
for animals.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
He's built up an email list of approximately 8,000 people.
He took his existing product, which is an e-book. It's an e-book and an audio
book bundled that normally sales for $45. This is just one of the offers he has.
What he did, was he offered it at nearly a 50% off sale. He also added in a couple
of bonuses. We'll talk about that in a minute.
Basically, this is a product that he says, some days he might have one or two
sales. Some days he has no sales, whatsoever. It's not like he was selling very much of
this. It's not like he had a heck of a lot to lose by discounting it.
What he did was, he added in a bonus special report and a one hour Q&A
webinar. He basically took this bundle that he already had for $45, wasn't hardly selling
at all, and then he added in a special report, a one-hour Q&A webinar, not too hard to do,
and he dropped the price to $25.
He did this as a four-day sale.
His results were $8,845. Not bad.
This is basically, for the cost of ... Well I guess he did put together a special report
and he's going to do a one-hour Q&A webinar. Pretty easy to do to get on a webinar and
answer questions for an hour.
He wrote six emails and he made almost $9,000.
Another key thing here is, if you've studied my work, especially like in Launching
Your List, the Launching Your List bonus course you get ... It's been my experience over
the years ... There's two primary types of lists. There's the list of prospects,
people that haven't bought from you yet, and a list of clients or customers,
who have bought from you. In my experience, someone who has bought from you,
and is on your client list, is worth fifteen times more on a per dollar basis
than someone who hasn't bought from you.
In other words, the next time you go to do a promotion, for every subscriber
that's on your client list, and you compare that to a subscriber on your prospect list, your
client list is going to generate fifteen times greater results.
You might have a 1,000-person prospect list and a 1,000 person client list. The
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

prospect list might generate $1,000 in sales and that customer list will generate $15,000
in sales. That has been my experience over and over in twenty years of business. It varies
on the offer. It can vary from business to business. Sometimes I've seen twelve or
thirteen times. Sometimes I've seen better than fifteen times.
What I'm getting at, is he now has 555 new clients. Of course, some of those
probably were already clients, but a lot of them were brand new clients. So, he not only
made almost $9,000 in sales, but he also generated 555 new people. He moved them
from the prospect list to the client list. Again, some of those are probably already on his
client list, but big chunk, let's say 400 of them, he moved from his prospect list to his
client list.
Of course, I'm just guessing at the number. I have no idea. But, that will have big
impact, big ramifications moving forward. It's always great to get people buying from
you.
Here's how the sales came in.
He did $875 the first day.
$1,600 the second day.
$3,500 the third day.
$2,300 the fourth day.
Then for people that came in afterwards, these people actually bought it at full
price, which is sort of interesting, another $520.
One interesting thing about this data is this last day wasn't all that. Normally you
see more sales on this final day than you would on any other day. I have a feeling that
one of the reasons is, he did this over the Memorial Day holiday. Which is a major
holiday here. A major summer holiday here in the United States. That might have
skewed the way his data fell out here. Not the data, but the way the orders came in and
why his fourth day was not the strongest of the bunch. Actually, I just realized I forgot
about this. I just realized why day four was down some. We'll get to that in just a minute.
Let's get on to how he did this. As you'll see, he sent out a quick Shot Across the
Bow. This went out in his normal weekly newsletter.

Then he came out with two emails on his final closing day.
One interesting thing here is, you know ... I just wonder, if Will had sent this out

generally works better. It generally works better. Sometimes, I'll make an exception.
Sometimes I'll send Friday afternoon, sometimes does pretty good for me. Sometimes,
Sunday afternoon, but in general I try to get my emails out earlier.
Here again, 5:30pm. Yeah. That's one thing I might .... Perhaps Will's done this,

Of course, you get this document with all these emails and everything, but this is

There's a few different themes roll through here. First of all, he tells them this

is leading into this whole idea of playing bigger.
He talks about being at a conference. I actually, he doesn't say, but I'm pretty sure
this is a PLF Live conference, where he had this great experience. An incredible
experience being around so many people there that were there to raise their game.
Brilliant people. How he felt he really had to get his message out.
Then, down here, he talks about making an investment and needing to raise some
capital to pay for that investment. This is one of the keys behind the Quick Launch. It's
generally story based. You're giving ... a story ends up landing on a reason why for the
discount.
Here, his reason why is:
I want to play a bigger role and part of that bigger role is bringing my message
out to more people. I want to bring that message to you and get you my message. Also,
part of that is I'm stepping up my game. I'm investing in my education so I can keep on
raising my game and continue to bring my work out there. Because of that I need to raise
money, so I'm going to do something very unusual. I've put together this deal for you.
You get nearly a 50% discount.
In this case. I think it probably; you know ... Will dropped it from $45 to $25. If
he would have dropped it from $45 to $22.50, then he could have just said it was a 50%
discount and for $2.50 per sale, it would make the copy more elegant and it might have
made ... People hear ... when you can just say half off. That's a powerful hook. For the
extra two and half dollars off, I probably would have just made it $22.50 instead of $25. I
cut the price in half for you.
Then he tells them about the extra stuff you're going to get. Okay. Then he
tells ... here's how you go get it. Here's how you go get it. Of course, it comes with a
guarantee. Thanks for being part of my tribe. That's it. A simple email. It's lengthy. You
can go through and read the whole thing, but one simple email and that starts the ball
rolling.
Then he comes back the next day, and this is actually part of his regular
newsletter, but if you don't do a regular newsletter, that doesn't matter. You can just
send this email. This is a four-day sale, basically, and you're going to want to send an
email each day, and two on the last day.
Here, he talks about the sale. He talks about the product. He talks about the

money you get off. It's that simple. It's just that simple. This is sort of a reminder; we've

If it was a stand-alone email ... this could totally work. Even though this is part of
his regular newsletter, you could do the same thing and have that be a stand-alone email.
This is a really simple additional email. Basically it's the last day of the sale. Talks a little
bit about why he's doing it. I'm fundraising to bring this natural health knowledge to the
world. A little bit about the extra bonuses you get, and that's it. This is, just sort of a final
reminder, we're closing soon.
This is where he changes things a little, because the next morning he sends out
this email with this subject line that says, "Duh, Just Duh", which I think is a pretty fun
subject. He said, "I didn't know what I was thinking, A lot of people said this is an
important sale, you're getting an important message out there, but everyone in the US is
outdoors enjoying Memorial Day. It was just pure oversight to close the sale when 57% of
you likely missed the alerts."
Basically, this is a pivot. He just realized that it wasn't a good day to close on
Memorial Day, so he extended one day and he gave his reason why. You can totally do
this. You can totally do this. This is the day, if we scroll way back up, this is the day that
he did the extra 141 orders. This was his original closing day. This was the day he sent
that email that said, duh I screwed up. He got an additional 141 orders by doing that.
Again, this is the “reason why” and that's like a copy writing term. When you have
this, what they call “reason why” copy, which is, you're telling people why you're doing
something. The day before he said we're closing, then he came back and said, yeah you
know what, I screwed up. I shouldn't have closed on a holiday here in the US, and so I
decided to extend it one more day. You can make the rules. You can totally do this.
The final email is right here. This is just the last warning. You really need to, on
your closing day, whatever launch you're doing, whether it's a see launch, or a Quick
Launch, an internal launch, a JV launch, you always have to have a least two emails on
your last day. That's what he did here. That's it.
This basically ... six email. Six emails and did almost $9,000 in sales. Generated
555 new clients and just did a fantastic Quick Launch.
